name: bioLogic
short_description: A shape-changing material and garment that uses living Bacillus subtilis natto bacteria as humidity-responsive nanoactuators to open ventilation flaps in response to body heat and sweat.
long_description: bioLogic introduces living Bacillus subtilis natto cells as humidity-sensitive nanoactuators: the cells expand and contract with atmospheric moisture, and when bio-printed onto flexible films they bend and curl, producing programmable shape change without motors or electronics. The flagship application, a 'Second Skin' garment, places these biofilm flaps over the body's heat zones so they open as the wearer sweats, enabling evaporative cooling. As physical AI and interaction design, bioLogic is significant for treating a living biological material as a directly actuated, environmentally responsive interface, framing shape-changing matter and the body itself as a computational and interactive medium.
ai_note: The actuation is a biological hygromorphic response of living natto cells to humidity, paired with computational design and fabrication tools; no AI/ML runs in the system itself.

images.caption: bioLogic 'Second Skin' garment with bacteria-actuated flaps that open over the body's heat zones in response to sweat.
images.alt_text: A close-fitting athletic garment whose fabric panels have curled-open flaps revealing the skin beneath, demonstrating moisture-triggered ventilation.

sources.title: bioLogic
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group

sources.title: bioLogic: Natto Cells as Nanoactuators for Shape Changing Interfaces
bioLogic: Natto Cells as Nanoactuators for Shape Changing Interfaces (DBLP record)

interactions.title: Sweat opens the back of your shirt
interactions.description: A runner heats up and starts to perspire, and the bacteria-coated flaps over the back's sweat zones curl open on their own, venting heat and letting moisture evaporate; as the skin cools and dries the flaps lie flat again.

interactions.title: A teabag that signals its own steeping
interactions.description: A living teabag changes shape in the hot water as the steam and heat reach the natto film, giving a physical cue about temperature and steeping rather than a printed timer.

interactions.title: Flowers that bloom and wilt with the air
interactions.description: An artificial plant made from the biofilm opens and closes its petals and shifts color as humidity rises and falls, blossoming when breathed on or in a damp room and curling shut as it dries.

interactions.title: A lampshade sculpted by the bulb's heat
interactions.description: The heat coming off a light bulb activates the bacterial film of a lampshade, making it flex and reshape and so change how the light is thrown into the room.

interactions.title: Origami toys that fold themselves when you breathe on them
interactions.description: Animated origami pieces printed with the natto film bend and reconfigure in response to moisture, so a person can drive the folding just by exhaling on them or holding them over steam.

interactions.title: Drawing the actuation with a bio-printer
interactions.description: A designer lays down living cells in patterned lines with the custom micron-resolution bioprinter, programming exactly where a flat film will bend or curl when humidity hits it — printing behavior, not motors.

interactions.title: Wound dressing that breathes with the skin
interactions.description: A bandage patterned with the humidity-responsive film could lift and vent over a sweaty or weeping area to release trapped moisture, then settle closed as the skin dries — a passive dressing that opens and shuts with no electronics.

interactions.title: A tent skin that ventilates itself
interactions.description: Camping fabric printed with the natto actuators could open its weave when the humid air inside builds up from breath and body heat overnight, then close again as the interior dries, regulating airflow without zips or fans.
